subject: Compliance Audits
title: Part 1 - SOX, COBIT, PCI DSS and HIPAA Map
description: A focused compliance map connecting SOX, COBIT, PCI DSS and HIPAA by protected data type, business purpose, shared controls, and framework relationships.

topics.title: Core Compliance Map
topics.definition: SOX, COBIT, PCI DSS and HIPAA occupy different places in the compliance ecosystem. SOX protects financial reporting, COBIT governs enterprise IT, PCI DSS protects cardholder data, and HIPAA protects health information.
keyPoints: SOX connects to financial reporting, internal controls, audit integrity, CEO and CFO accountability, and PCAOB oversight.
COBIT connects to IT governance, risk, controls, processes, and maturity.
PCI DSS connects to cardholder data protection, payment security, merchants, service providers, the 12 requirements, and PCI SSC.
HIPAA connects to Protected Health Information, privacy, security, breach notification, healthcare providers, health plans, and business associates.
workflows: Compliance -> PCI DSS: Cardholder Data Protection
oneLiners: Each framework protects a different business interest.

topics.title: High-Level Mental Model
topics.definition: The four frameworks should be understood by type, protected subject, and applicability.
keyPoints: SOX is a United States federal law protecting financial reporting for public companies and applicable entities.
COBIT is a governance framework for enterprise IT governance and can be used by any enterprise.
PCI DSS is an industry security standard for payment card data and applies to merchants and payment service providers.
HIPAA is a United States federal law protecting PHI and applies to healthcare organizations and related business associates.
comparisons: PCI DSS = standard for cardholder data.
oneLiners: Type, protected data, and applicability separate the frameworks.

topics.title: SOX Summary
topics.definition: SOX focuses on protecting investors through reliable financial reporting, internal controls, management accountability, and audit integrity.
keyPoints: SOX is centered on financial statements and financial-reporting reliability.
Internal controls support accuracy, completeness, authorization, and auditability.
CEO and CFO accountability is a key governance feature.
PCAOB oversight supports external audit quality.
IT systems become SOX-relevant when they affect financial data or reporting.
workflows: Financial Reporting -> Internal Controls -> Audit Evidence -> Management Certification -> SOX Compliance
oneLiners: SOX protects trust in financial reporting.

topics.title: COBIT Summary
topics.definition: COBIT is an IT governance framework that helps organize governance, risk, control objectives, processes, responsibilities, measurement, and maturity.
keyPoints: COBIT connects IT activities with business goals.
It helps define and measure controls.
It supports governance and management responsibilities.
It provides maturity and improvement thinking.
COBIT can support implementation of controls required by other compliance obligations.
workflows: Business Requirements -> IT Governance -> Control Objectives -> Processes -> Metrics -> Maturity Improvement
oneLiners: COBIT makes IT governance structured and measurable.

topics.title: PCI DSS Summary
topics.definition: PCI DSS protects cardholder data and payment systems through a structured set of payment-security requirements.
keyPoints: PCI DSS focuses on payment card data.
It applies to merchants and service providers that store, process, or transmit payment-card account data.
The PCI Security Standards Council maintains the standard.
Common control themes include firewalls, encryption, access control, monitoring, and security testing.
PCI DSS uses defense in depth; one control does not replace all other requirements.
workflows: Sensitive Data -> Payment Card Data -> Cardholder Protection -> Firewalls -> Encryption -> Access Control -> Monitoring -> Security Testing
oneLiners: PCI DSS follows the card data.

topics.title: HIPAA Summary
topics.definition: HIPAA protects Protected Health Information through privacy, security, and breach-notification requirements.
keyPoints: HIPAA focuses on PHI and electronic PHI.
It applies to healthcare providers, health plans, and business associates where applicable.
Privacy requirements govern appropriate use and disclosure of PHI.
Security requirements protect electronic PHI through administrative, technical, and physical safeguards.
Breach notification requirements support accountability after certain incidents.
workflows: Sensitive Data -> Health Information -> PHI -> Patient Privacy -> Administrative Safeguards -> Technical Safeguards -> Physical Safeguards
oneLiners: HIPAA protects patient information and healthcare privacy.

topics.title: SOX and COBIT Connection
topics.definition: SOX creates expectations for effective financial controls, while COBIT helps structure IT governance and controls that support those expectations.
keyPoints: SOX says financial controls must be effective.
COBIT explains how IT governance and controls can be built, organized, measured, and improved.
COBIT can help translate SOX control needs into IT control objectives, processes, owners, evidence, and monitoring.
workflows: SOX Financial-Control Requirement -> IT Dependency -> COBIT Control Objective -> IT Control -> Evidence -> Assurance
oneLiners: SOX defines accountability; COBIT helps govern IT controls.

topics.title: PCI DSS Control Chain
topics.definition: PCI DSS begins with sensitive payment card data and builds layered controls around the cardholder-data environment.
keyPoints: Cardholder information must be protected during storage, processing, and transmission.
Firewalls reduce unnecessary exposure.
Encryption protects sensitive data from disclosure.
Access control limits who can reach payment systems.
Monitoring and logging help detect suspicious activity.
Security testing verifies whether controls remain effective.
workflows: Payment Card Data -> Protect Cardholder Information -> Firewalls -> Encryption -> Access Control -> Monitoring -> Security Testing
oneLiners: PCI DSS protects payment data through layered controls.

topics.title: HIPAA Safeguard Chain
topics.definition: HIPAA connects health information to privacy and security safeguards that protect patients and healthcare data.
keyPoints: PHI requires controlled access and appropriate use.
Administrative safeguards include policies, workforce training, risk management, and governance.
Technical safeguards include access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, and transmission security.
Physical safeguards protect facilities, workstations, devices, and media.
workflows: Health Information -> PHI -> Patient Privacy -> Administrative Safeguards -> Technical Safeguards -> Physical Safeguards
oneLiners: HIPAA turns patient privacy into safeguards.

topics.title: Separation by Data Type
topics.definition: A practical way to identify the relevant compliance area is to start with the data or business process being protected.
keyPoints: Financial statements map to SOX.
Enterprise IT governance maps to COBIT.
Credit card data maps to PCI DSS.
Patient health data maps to HIPAA.
oneLiners: Start with the data type to identify the framework.

topics.title: Unified Compliance Stack
topics.definition: An enterprise can organize these frameworks into governance, financial compliance, payment compliance, and healthcare compliance layers.
keyPoints: COBIT supports the governance layer.
SOX supports financial compliance.
PCI DSS supports payment compliance.
HIPAA supports healthcare compliance.
The same organization may need more than one framework if it processes multiple data types or performs multiple regulated activities.
oneLiners: One enterprise can have multiple compliance layers.

topics.title: Core Security Objectives
topics.definition: Each framework has a different primary objective, but all depend on controls and evidence.
keyPoints: SOX protects financial reporting, internal controls, and audit integrity.
COBIT manages IT governance, risk, business alignment, and control objectives.
PCI DSS protects cardholder data, payment systems, and transaction security.
HIPAA protects PHI, patient privacy, and electronic health records.
oneLiners: The framework objective changes; the need for control evidence remains.

topics.title: Common Controls Across Frameworks
topics.definition: SOX, COBIT, PCI DSS and HIPAA often rely on similar control families, even though their protected interests differ.
keyPoints: Common controls include identity management.
Least privilege limits unnecessary access.
Authentication verifies users.
Logging and monitoring provide visibility.
Encryption protects sensitive data.
Risk assessment prioritizes controls.
Policies define expected behavior.
Incident response handles failures.
Regular audits provide assurance.
workflows: Identity Management -> Least Privilege -> Authentication -> Logging -> Monitoring -> Encryption -> Risk Assessment -> Policies -> Incident Response -> Regular Audits
oneLiners: Shared controls support different compliance goals.

topics.title: Encryption as a Shared Control
topics.definition: Encryption is a shared security control whose compliance meaning depends on the protected data type.
keyPoints: Encryption protects financial reports or supporting data under SOX when confidentiality and integrity matter.
Encryption protects cardholder data under PCI DSS.
Encryption protects electronic PHI under HIPAA.
COBIT recommends governance around selecting, implementing, managing, and monitoring encryption controls.
oneLiners: The same control can satisfy different compliance needs when mapped correctly.

topics.title: Data Classification Map
topics.definition: Data classification links information types to the most relevant compliance framework or governance model.
keyPoints: Financial data maps to SOX.
Cardholder data maps to PCI DSS.
Protected Health Information maps to HIPAA.
Business information maps to COBIT governance.
oneLiners: Classification tells the organization which obligations matter.

topics.title: CIA Framework Relationships
topics.definition: The CIA Triad appears across all four frameworks, but different frameworks emphasize different pillars.
keyPoints: HIPAA strongly emphasizes confidentiality because health information is sensitive.
SOX strongly emphasizes integrity because financial statements must be accurate and reliable.
COBIT strongly supports availability and governance because enterprise IT must support business objectives.
PCI DSS uses confidentiality, integrity, and availability together because payment systems must protect and process cardholder data securely.
workflows: CIA Triad -> Availability and Governance -> COBIT
CIA Triad -> Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability -> PCI DSS
oneLiners: CIA is the security language under the compliance map.

topics.title: Compliance Memory Hook
topics.definition: A short memory hook links each framework to its most recognizable domain.
keyPoints: Money maps to SOX.
IT governance maps to COBIT.
Cards map to PCI DSS.
Patients map to HIPAA.
oneLiners: Money, IT, cards, patients.

topics.title: Knowledge Graph
topics.definition: The compliance knowledge graph organizes frameworks into governance, financial, payment and healthcare branches.
keyPoints: Governance connects to COBIT.
Financial compliance connects to SOX.
Payment compliance connects to PCI DSS.
Healthcare compliance connects to HIPAA.
The graph helps build comparisons, scenario reasoning, and control mapping.
oneLiners: A knowledge graph turns isolated frameworks into connected reasoning.

topics.title: Control Mapping Method
topics.definition: Control mapping explains how one security control can support multiple frameworks when tied to the right data type and requirement.
keyPoints: Identify the data or process.
Identify the applicable framework.
Identify the relevant control objective.
Select the security control.
Collect evidence that the control is designed and operating.
Review and improve the control over time.
workflows: Data Type -> Framework -> Control Objective -> Security Control -> Evidence -> Review
oneLiners: Controls become compliance evidence only when mapped to requirements.

topics.title: Scenario Reasoning
topics.definition: Scenario-based compliance reasoning starts with the asset or data, then identifies the applicable framework and supporting controls.
keyPoints: If the scenario involves public-company financial reporting, consider SOX.
If it involves enterprise IT governance and control maturity, consider COBIT.
If it involves cardholder data or payment systems, consider PCI DSS.
If it involves PHI, patient privacy, or healthcare safeguards, consider HIPAA.
If a scenario includes several data types, more than one framework may apply.
workflows: Scenario -> Data Type -> Business Context -> Framework -> Controls -> Evidence
oneLiners: Scenario questions are solved by identifying the protected data and business context.

topics.title: Final Memory Map
topics.definition: SOX, COBIT, PCI DSS and HIPAA form a compact compliance map: money, IT, cards and patients.
keyPoints: SOX means money and financial-reporting reliability.
COBIT means IT governance and control maturity.
PCI DSS means cards and payment security.
HIPAA means patients and protected health information.
Shared controls include identity, least privilege, authentication, logging, monitoring, encryption, risk assessment, policies, incident response and audits.
workflows: Cards -> PCI DSS -> Payment Security Controls
Patients -> HIPAA -> Healthcare Privacy and Security Safeguards
oneLiners: Money, IT, cards and patients summarize the four-framework map.
